1: koh amar Adonai YHWH sha'ar hechatzer hapnimit ('thus says the Lord YHWH: the gate of the inner court'): the gate regulations for the east gate of the inner court (not the outer east gate of 44:1-2). The six working days/two exceptional days principle: sheshet yamei hama'aseh yisager ('on the six days of work it shall be shut') and veyom hashabbat yippatach ('but on the day of Sabbath it shall be opened') — the Sabbath and New Moon as the days of open access to the inner court.
2: uva hanasi derekh ulam hasha'ar mibachutz ('and the prince shall enter by the vestibule of the gate from outside'): the prince's specific entry route. Ve'amad al-mezuzat hasha'ar ('he shall stand at the doorpost of the gate'): the prince as a liminal figure — standing at the threshold between common and sacred space. He does not enter the sanctuary but stands at its gate. The priests prepare his offerings; he oversees from the threshold. His Sabbath and New Moon worship is described in detail (vv. 4-7).
3: vehishtachavu am ha'aretz petach hasha'ar hazeh ('and the people of the land shall worship at the entrance of that gate'): the congregation worships at the outer gate while the prince stands at the inner gate. The concentric model of sacred access: prince closer to the inner court than the people, but neither entering the sanctuary itself. The spatial theology of access matches covenantal proximity: the people gather at the outer perimeter of sacred space.
4: vehha'olah asher-yaqrib hanasi laYHWH beyom hashabbat ('and the burnt offering that the prince shall bring to YHWH on the Sabbath day'): six lambs and a ram without blemish — the Sabbath offering in the new temple order. The Sabbath offering increases dramatically from the Torah's two lambs (Num 28:9-10) to six lambs plus a ram — the eschatological intensification of Sabbath worship. Tamim (without blemish) governs all the animals.
5: vehamminchah la'ayil ('and the grain offering for the ram'): the grain accompaniment. Ve'likhvasim minchah matnat yado ('and for the lambs, a grain offering as his hand gives freely'): matnat yado (the gift of his hand) — a freewill element in the specified quantity. The fixed animal offerings have a flexible grain component. Vehin shemen la'efah: the oil portion consistently specified.
6: uveyom hachodesh par ben-baqar tamim ('and on the day of the new moon, a young bull without blemish'): the New Moon offerings — a bull, six lambs, and a ram. The New Moon (Rosh Chodesh) is elevated to an occasion of substantial sacrifice alongside the Sabbath. Sheishet kevasim ve'ayil tamim yihyu: the animal list. The New Moon as a covenant calendar marker is given structural importance in the new temple order.
7: uminchah efah lapar ('and a grain offering — an ephah for the bull'): the proportionate grain offerings. The flexibility noted in v.5 — matnat yado for the lambs — reappears here. The prescribed quantities for bulls and rams are fixed; for lambs the grain offering is proportionate to what the prince can give. The freewill element within the regulated system balances obligation and devotion.
8: uvebo hanasi derekh ulam hasha'ar yavo ('and when the prince enters, he shall enter by the vestibule of the gate'): the specific path for the prince's entry and exit — always through the vestibule (ulam). Uvedarko yetze' ('and by the same way he shall go out'): unlike the general worshippers (vv. 9-10) who enter one gate and exit another, the prince enters and exits by the same route — perhaps indicating his distinctive mediating role between the people and the sanctuary.
9: uvevo am ha'aretz lifnei YHWH bammo'adim ('when the people of the land come before YHWH at the appointed feasts'): the one-directional flow rule for the people at festivals — entry through the north gate means exit through the south gate, and vice versa. Yotze derekh-sha'ar nagevu ('shall go out by the way of the south gate'). The traffic management of worshippers prevents congestion and ensures orderly, reverent movement through sacred space. The directional regulation also has symbolic weight: forward movement through the sanctuary, not retreat.
10: vehannasi betocham bevo'am yavo uvetzetam yetze'u ('and the prince, among them — when they enter, he enters, and when they go out, he goes out'): the prince as the representative member of the worshipping community. He does not precede or follow the people as a separate dignitary but moves with them (betocham, among them). The communal nature of the prince's worship is emphasized — he is the first among worshippers, not a separate liturgical class.
11: uvachagim uva'mo'adim tihyeh hamincha efah lapar ('and at festivals and at appointed feasts, the grain offering shall be an ephah for the bull'): the festival grain-offerings specification repeated for the general festival context. The grain offerings follow the same structure as the specific festival prescriptions in chapter 45 — a unified system across all sacred occasions. Matnat yado for the lambs again — the freewill element in the lamb-grain offering preserved.
12: uchi ya'aseh hanasi nedavah ('and when the prince makes a freewill offering'): the freewill offering (nedavah — from nadav, to volunteer/give spontaneously) is specified separately from the required offerings. The east inner gate is opened specifically for the prince's freewill offering; after he exits the gate is closed again. The opening and closing of the gate for the freewill offering signals that spontaneous devotion, not merely calendar obligation, has its own access to the sacred space.
13: keves ben-shnato tamim ta'aseh olah laYHWH laboqer boqer ('a lamb a year old without blemish you shall prepare as a burnt offering to YHWH morning by morning'): the daily burnt offering — one lamb each morning, not the two lambs of Numbers 28:3-4. The evening offering (tamid) is omitted in Ezekiel's vision — only the morning daily. This has generated discussion: perhaps the vision is selective, emphasizing a continuous morning orientation toward YHWH rather than prescribing the complete Levitical daily rhythm.
14: uminchah ta'aseh alav baboqer ('and a grain offering you shall prepare with it in the morning'): the accompanying grain offering — a sixth of an ephah of fine flour (solet) with a third of a hin of oil. Mincha tamid olat tamid laYHWH ('a continual grain offering, a continual burnt offering for YHWH'): the tamid (continual) characterizes both offerings — the daily morning worship is perpetual, not occasional. The rhythm of boqer boqer (morning by morning) establishes the tempo of the new temple's liturgical life.
15: ya'asu et-hakeves ve'et-hamminchah ve'et-hashemen baboqer boqer ('they shall prepare the lamb and the grain offering and the oil — morning by morning'): the triple repetition of the morning-orientation confirms the continuous daily worship at the heart of the new temple order. The eschatological temple has a daily rhythm that is simple (one lamb), perpetual (tamid), and consistently oriented toward the morning — the beginning of each new day acknowledged as YHWH's gift.
16: koh amar Adonai YHWH ki-yitten hanasi mattana la'echad mivanav ('thus says the Lord YHWH: if the prince gives a gift to any of his sons'): the inheritance principle for the prince's land. Gifts to sons become their permanent inheritance (achuztam hi birushasha lahem). But gifts to servants can only be held until the jubilee year (shnat haderut), after which they revert. The jubilee principle from Leviticus 25 is maintained in the new order: land cannot be permanently alienated through servitude.
17: ve'im-yitten mattana me'nachalato le'echad me'avadav ('but if he gives a gift from his inheritance to one of his servants'): the servant case. Vehayetah lo ad-shnat haderut veshavah lanasi ('it shall be his until the year of release/jubilee and shall return to the prince'): the jubilee reversion — the servant does not permanently acquire the prince's land. Ach nachalato lava'nav tihyeh lahem ('only his inheritance shall be for his sons to them'): the prince's land is held in trust for his descendants, not permanently dispersible through gift to servants. This provision prevents the prince from accumulating or dispersing land in ways that destabilize the tribal land inheritance.
18: velo-yiqach hanasi min-nachala ha'am ('and the prince shall not take from the people's inheritance'): the direct prohibition on the Naboth's-vineyard type of royal confiscation. Lehona'am me'achuzatam ('to oppress them out of their possession'): hona (to oppress, especially economically) — the specific verb for the economic exploitation the Israelite kings regularly practiced. Me'achuztam ('from their possession'): each family's ancestral land holding is protected from royal appropriation. The prince's portion is specified (45:7-8) precisely so he will not need to take from others.
19: vayvi'eni bemavo asher al-ketef hasha'ar el-leshkot haQodesh ('and he brought me through the entrance that was at the side of the gate into the priestly chambers'): the vision guide brings Ezekiel to the temple kitchens. The two sets of kitchen facilities — for the priests (vv. 19-20) and for the Levitical servants (vv. 21-24) — reflect the two-tier sacred service structure of chapters 44-45.
20: zeh hamaqom asher yevashelu sham hakohanim et-ha'asham ve'et-hachatta't ('this is the place where the priests shall boil the guilt offering and the sin offering'): the sacred kitchen where the priests' portion of the guilt and sin offerings is cooked. Veyafeu et-hammincha leBelti hotzi el-hechatzer hachitzona ('and where they shall bake the grain offering, in order not to bring them out to the outer court'): the isolation principle — the offerings that belong to the priests must be cooked and eaten within the priestly zone. The holiness of the sacrificial food must not be allowed to 'sanctify the people' by contact — the same principle as the garment-holiness of 44:19.
21: vatze'eni el-hechatzer hachitzona ('and he brought me out to the outer court'): transition from the priestly inner area to the outer court, where the Levitical servants' kitchen facilities are located. Four corner areas in the outer court, each with a small enclosed court. Bechot yot hechatzer chatzerot qetanot ('in the corners of the court were small courts'): the four enclosed areas symmetrically placed.
22: arba machzurot bearbaat pinnot hechatzer ('four joined/enclosed courts in the four corners of the court'): machzurot (enclosed/walled courts) — the small kitchen areas. Arba'im orech ushEloshim rochav midda achat learba'tam ('forty long and thirty wide, one measure for all four'): uniform size, symmetrical placement. The architectural regularity of the sacred space is a consistent feature of Ezekiel's temple vision — perfect symmetry expressing divine order.
23: turo sav lahem saviv learba'tam ('a course of stone was around them for all four'): the construction detail of the kitchen enclosures. Umevushillot asuyot tachat haturim saviv ('and cooking-hearths made under the courses/ledges around'): the cooking facilities built into the stone structure. The detail suggests functional kitchens fully integrated into the architectural plan — worship includes the physical preparation of food, and that preparation has its proper designated space.
24: vayomer elai eleh beit hamevashllim asher yevashelu sham meshartei habayit et-zevach ha'am ('and he said to me: these are the houses of boiling where the servants of the house shall boil the sacrifice of the people'): the Levitical servants cook the people's portions of the peace offerings here. The chapter — and the liturgical regulation section of the temple vision — closes with a picture of shared communal meal: priests eating their portions of sin and guilt offerings in the inner priestly kitchen, people's peace offerings cooked in the outer Levitical kitchen, the entire community sustained by the sacrificial system that YHWH has ordained. The temple is not merely a ritual performance space but a place of communal feasting in the presence of YHWH.
